items.title: 4 years of war in Ukraine: by the numbers
items.summary_auto: Russia's invasion of Ukraine four years ago launched Europe's biggest conflict since the Second World War, causing immense suffering for civilians and harrowing ordeals for soldiers while rewriting the post-Cold War security order.

items.title: Canadian Olympians land back at home after Milano Cortina Games
items.summary_auto: Canada's Olympic athletes began arriving home Monday greeted by friends, family and fans as they returned from the Milano Cortina 2026 Olympics.

items.title: U.S. military says strike on alleged drug boat kills 3 in Caribbean Sea
items.summary_auto: The U.S. military said it killed three people Monday in a strike on an alleged drug-smuggling vessel in the Caribbean Sea as part of the Trump administration's months-long campaign against alleged traffickers.

items.title: One of Mexico's most-wanted drug lords killed | About That
items.summary_auto: Andrew Chang explains the fallout after the Mexican military killed Nemesio 'El Mencho' Oseguera Cervantes, and how the cartel leader's death sparked widespread unrest across Mexico. Images provided by The Canadian Press, Reuters and Getty Images

items.title: 'I think we'll have enough space' in office for public servants 4 days a week: Treasury Board president
items.summary_auto: Shafqat Ali's Monday statement comes only days after the treasury board suggested the opposite to Radio-Canada.

items.title: Liberals accept Conservative proposals to limit 'immense' cabinet powers in budget bill
items.summary_auto: The federal Liberals have accepted amendments to the most controversial section of the Carney government's budget bill, effectively putting guardrails on new proposed powers that would allow the government to grant corporations the ability to bypass existing laws and regulations.

items.title: Toronto man donates $10 million to expand OCD treatment centre that changed his life
items.summary_auto: For decades, Brian Reeve has lived with obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD). Seven years ago, he hit a breaking point when the time-consuming rituals began interfering with his daily life and ability to function. After seeking treatment at the Frederick W. Thompson Anxiety Disorders Centre, he is now in remission and giving back through a large donation.

items.title: Fact check: Danielle Smith’s comments on temporary residents
items.summary_auto: Alberta Premier Danielle Smith is defending a plan to limit health and education support for temporary residents. We fact-checked some of Smith’s claims about temporary foreign workers and Alberta taxpayers.

items.title: Senate committee recommends removing broad immigration powers from border bill
items.summary_auto: Senators on the social affairs committee want to see immigration-related sections in the government's border bill, C-12, removed or significantly modified by the Senate national security committee.

items.title: Why removing Andrew from the line of succession is not as easy as it sounds
CONCACAF monitoring violence in Mexico as Champions Cup continues
items.summary_auto: Soccer's governing body in North America says it's closely monitoring ongoing violence in Mexico as its international tournament, the 27-club Champions Cup, continues.

items.title: Where Trump sees big 'ugly' windmills, Canada sees opportunity
items.summary_auto: U.S. President Trump calls wind power projects "ugly losers," and it's putting a deep chill on investments. But Canada stands to benefit as wind companies consider new prospects off the East Coast.

items.title: Federal government seeking input to develop men's and boys' health strategy
items.summary_auto: The federal government is launching a "national conversation" to inform a future strategy on men's and boys' health.

items.title: U.S. tells some of its diplomats and their families to leave Lebanon amid Iran tensions
items.summary_auto: The United States has ordered non-essential diplomats and their family members at the U.S. Embassy in Beirut to leave Lebanon, the State Department said Monday, as tensions over Iran rise with the threat of a potentially imminent military strike.

items.title: Olympic ice dance medallists Gilles, Poirier headline Canadian world championship team
items.summary_auto: Newly minted Olympic bronze medallist ice dancers Piper Gilles and Paul Poirier will be part of the Canadian contingent at the figure skating world championships in March. The pair will be joined by Stephen Gogolev, Madeline Schizas, Lia Pereira and Trennt Michaud.

items.title: NDP's lone Quebec MP says he's considering jump to provincial politics
items.summary_auto: Quebec NDP MP Alexandre Boulerice said Monday that he's seriously considering running for the Québec Solidaire in this year's provincial election.

items.title: N.S. budget contains major cuts as province confronts $1.2B deficit
items.summary_auto: About 1,000 full-time equivalent jobs could come off the books this year.

items.title: International Paralympic Committee stands firm on Russian athletes competing amid boycott warnings
items.summary_auto: Russian and Belarusian athletes will be allowed to compete at the Winter Paralympics under their flags and with their national anthems following a decision by the International Paralympic Committee General Assembly, its ‌President Andrew Parsons said Monday.

items.title: IN PHOTOS | Snowstorm hits northeastern U.S.
items.summary_auto: Low visibility along with high winds and blizzard warnings have stalled New York as residents brace for power outages.

items.title: Who was 'El Mencho,' the just-killed leader of a deadly Mexican cartel?
items.summary_auto: The killing of Nemesio Rubén Oseguera Cervantes in an operation in Jalisco state is likely sending "psychological shockwaves" through Mexico's drug cartels, a former high-place U.S. official told CBC News on Monday.

items.title: Ford says 'walls closing in' on Trump after SCOTUS strikes down tariffs
items.summary_auto: Ontario Premier Doug Ford told reporters at Queen's Park Monday that the decision last week was a "positive message from the [U.S.] Supreme Court,” though tariffs are still in place on sectors important to the province, including auto and steel.

items.title: Ex-ambassador Peter Mandelson arrested in U.K. misconduct probe tied to Epstein files
items.summary_auto: British police have arrested Peter Mandelson, a former U.K. ambassador to the United States, in a misconduct probe stemming from his ties with Jeffrey Epstein.

items.title: Moroun donated $1M US to Trump-supporting super PAC before Gordie Howe bridge threats
items.summary_auto: The wealthy owner of a nearly century-old bridge connecting Michigan and Ontario gave $1 million US to a Trump-aligned political action committee less than a month before the U.S. president unleashed an online tirade against a new competing span down the Detroit River, records show.

items.title: Travel disrupted as nor'easter blasts Nova Scotia with snow, wind
British film academy, BBC apologize after BAFTA awards guest with Tourette's shouts racial slur
items.summary_auto: The Tourette syndrome campaigner who shouted the slur said he was "deeply mortified" and what he said was "not a reflection of my personal beliefs."

items.title: FBI identifies gunman shot dead at entrance of Trump's Florida estate
items.summary_auto: The FBI and U.S. Secret Service have released more details after an armed man drove into the secure perimeter of Mar-a-Lago, President Donald Trump's resort in Palm Beach, Fla., on Sunday morning. He was identified as Austin Martin, 21, a North Carolina native.

items.title: Nick Reiner pleads not guilty to murder in the killing of his parents
items.summary_auto: Nick Reiner pleaded not guilty Monday to two counts of first-degree murder in the killing of his parents, Rob Reiner and Michele Singer Reiner.

items.title: Measles outbreak not a concern for many in Winkler-Morden area
items.summary_auto: Despite high measles rates in the Winkler and Morden area of southern Manitoba, many residents remain unconcerned about the illness, CBC News found during a recent visit.

items.title: Schools to remain closed Tuesday as snow cleanup continues in St. John's metro
items.summary_auto: Schools are closed again on Tuesday, and the City of St. John's is asking residents to stay off the roads as much as possible while plows attempt to clean up the latest dumping of snow — pushing the city towards 100 cm of snow in less than a week.

items.title: 'We have failed them': Studies overlook sex, gender in veteran pain care
items.summary_auto: A recent study on chronic pain management for Canadian veterans has uncovered a critical flaw in decades of research: Not a single one examined the impact of sex or gender on treatment effectiveness.

items.title: Travel bans, flight cancellations pile up as winter storm blasts U.S. northeast
items.summary_auto: Millions of people in New York City and a large swath of the northeastern U.S. were stuck at home under road travel bans and blizzard warnings Monday as heavy snow and strong winds intensified, creating whiteout conditions in the densely populated region.

items.title: Edmonton woman frustrated by 18-month battle with Equifax and TransUnion to fix credit rating
items.summary_auto: After identity thieves racked up $20,000 in debt in her name, Zoe Lorenz-Boser spent a year and a half trying to convince Equifax and TransUnion the debts weren’t hers. Experts say her case highlights systemic problems with Canada’s credit reporting system.

items.title: Nunavut gov’t says no police resources will be used on federal gun buyback program
items.summary_auto: The Nunavut government says it will not be using police resources on the federal gun buyback program, and solutions proposed by Ottawa do not apply to the territory.

items.title: An Alberta man wanted MAID. Instead, he died in a Catholic hospital, waiting to be transferred
items.summary_auto: William Hume knew he was dying and wanted medical assistance in dying (MAID). But when he ended up in a hospital run by a Catholic health-care provider, his last wishes weren't able to happen. He would die while waiting to transfer so he could receive MAID.

items.title: If food is medicine, how about a prescription for blueberries? N.S. researchers hope that’s the future
items.summary_auto: A new study is examining whether providing blueberries for people to eat every day, combined with 30 grams of protein powder and personalized exercise routines, will improve frailty and heart health in older people. If it does, researchers hope the results will bolster the argument for doctors to prescribe blueberries.

items.title: Doctors worried amid rise in western Quebec patients being transferred elsewhere
items.summary_auto: Dozens of western Quebec patients were transferred elsewhere for medical care last year, leading to concerns that Outaouais residents could be at increased risk if they're seriously injured in an accident.
